attractions.name: BisMan Community Food Co-op
attractions.description: BisMan Community Food Co-op was a member-owned grocery store dedicated to providing the Bismarck-Mandan area with access to fresh, local, and organic foods. It served as a community hub featuring a full-service deli, bakery, and cafe that highlighted products from over 70 local producers. Although the physical storefront has closed, it remains a celebrated example of cooperative enterprise in North Dakota.

attractions.name: Bismarck Parks and Recreation District
attractions.description: The Bismarck Parks and Recreation District (BPRD) serves the City of Bismarck and Burleigh County by providing high-quality parks, programs, and facilities. Established in 1927, the district manages thousands of acres of park land and an extensive network of recreational trails. Residents and visitors can enjoy year-round activities ranging from aquatic centers and ice arenas to seasonal community events and youth programs (Bismarck Parks & Recreation District).

It later served as a U.S. Weather Bureau station and now functions as an interpretive museum showcasing local history and artifacts (ND Tourism, Wikipedia).

Occupied between the 15th and 18th centuries, the park preserves the remains of earthlodges and a fortification ditch, offering a self-guided interpretive trail for visitors. Bismarck Parks and Recreation, Daily Kos

attractions.name: Cross Ranch Nature Preserve
attractions.description: Cross Ranch Nature Preserve is a sprawling 6,000-acre protected area managed by The Nature Conservancy, featuring one of the last free-flowing stretches of the Missouri River in North Dakota. The landscape consists of untouched mixed-grass prairie, river bottom forests, and rugged woody draws that provide a primitive outdoor experience similar to what Lewis and Clark encountered in 1804.
attractions.hours: Open daily from dawn to dusk.
attractions.days_open: Daily
attractions.admission: Free
highlights: ['Home to a resident conservation herd of over 200 American bison.', 'Contains one of the few remaining free-flowing and undeveloped sections of the Missouri River.', 'Features over 16 miles of primitive trails for hiking, birdwatching, and cross-country skiing.', "Critical habitat for rare grassland birds such as Sprague's pipit and Baird's sparrow.", 'Pristine mixed-grass prairie landscape with native wildflowers and ancient cottonwood forests.', 'Access to a boat ramp for exploring the scenic river segment via kayak or canoe.']
attractions.tips: Visitors are advised to remain on marked trails as bison roam freely and can be dangerous; bring binoculars for birdwatching and sturdy hiking shoes. While the preserve has no public facilities, restrooms and picnic areas are available at the adjacent Cross Ranch State Park.
attractions.nearby: Cross Ranch State Park (adjacent), Knife River Indian Villages National Historic Site, Washburn (7 miles north), Garrison Dam.

attractions.name: Custer House at Fort Abraham Lincoln State Park
attractions.description: The Custer House is a meticulously reconstructed Victorian-style home that served as the final residence of General George Armstrong Custer and his wife, Libbie. Visitors can experience "living history" tours led by docents in period costumes who portray the inhabitants of the fort as they lived in 1875.
attractions.hours: Visitor Center: Open Daily 9:00 AM – 5:00 PM (May–October); Tours: Typically held on the hour (excluding 12:00 PM) during the summer season.
attractions.days_open: Daily (Tours available Memorial Day weekend through Labor Day)
attractions.admission: $7 vehicle entrance fee per day; Interpretive Tour Pass (includes Custer House and On-A-Slant Village): Adult $8, Student (K-12) $5
highlights: ["Guided living history tours of the reconstructed 1875 Commanding Officer's Quarters", 'Authentic Victorian-style furnishings and period-accurate architecture', 'Part of a larger historic site including the reconstructed On-A-Slant Mandan Indian Village', 'Scenic views of the Missouri and Heart River valleys from the surrounding hills', 'Access to other reconstructed military buildings such as the barracks, granary, and stables', 'Interpretive museum at the Visitor Center featuring Mandan and military artifacts']
attractions.tips: Purchase interpretive tour passes at the Visitor Center or Commissary before heading to the house. Tours are conducted on the hour, so plan your arrival to coordinate with the schedule; the house tour typically lasts about 30–45 minutes.

attractions.name: Double Ditch Indian Village State Historic Site
attractions.description: Double Ditch Indian Village was a large earthlodge village inhabited by the Mandan Indians for nearly 300 years, from approximately AD 1490 to 1785. The site preserves the ruins of this vibrant trading center, which once housed thousands of people before being abandoned following a massive smallpox epidemic. (State Historical Society of North Dakota, Wikipedia)

attractions.tips: Visit during warmer months for better hiking conditions; bring a camera for the stunning river views and unique archaeological features; take time to read all interpretive signage to fully appreciate the Mandan cultural legacy. (Evendo, Only In Your State)

attractions.name: Former Governors' Mansion State Historic Site
attractions.description: The Former Governors' Mansion State Historic Site is a restored 1884 Victorian Stick Style house that served as the official residence for 20 North Dakota governors from 1893 to 1960. Originally built as a private home for businessman Asa Fisher, it now functions as a museum featuring unique room exhibits that highlight the restoration process, architectural changes, and period furniture. attractions.name: Petrified Forest Area, Theodore Roosevelt National Park
attractions.description: The Petrified Forest Area in Theodore Roosevelt National Park contains the third highest concentration of petrified wood in the United States. This remote wilderness region features massive stumps of ancient sequoia-like trees that were fossilized over 60 million years ago when the landscape was a subtropical swampy delta.
attractions.address: 201 East River Road North (South Unit Visitor Center) / Trailhead via Forest Service Road 730
attractions.region: South Unit, Theodore Roosevelt National Park
attractions.hours: Open 24 hours daily; Visitor Center hours vary by season (typically 8:30 AM - 4:30 PM or 5:30 PM)
attractions.days_open: Daily (Year-round)
attractions.admission: $30 per private vehicle (7-day pass), $25 per motorcycle, $15 per person (walk-in/bicycle). $55 TRNP Annual Pass, $80 America the Beautiful Pass.
highlights: ['Massive petrified sequoia stumps up to 12 feet in diameter', 'Breathtaking badlands scenery and expansive prairie views', 'Frequent sightings of bison, wild horses, and elk in their natural habitat', 'Access to the 144-mile Maah Daah Hey Trail system', 'Remote wilderness character offering profound solitude and quiet', "Unique geologic formations across the 'Big Plateau' region"]
attractions.tips: Bring at least 2 liters of water per person as there is no shade or water available on the trail. The access roads are gravel/dirt and can become impassable when wet; always check current weather and road conditions with a ranger before heading out. It is strictly illegal to collect or remove any petrified wood from the park.

attractions.name: Heart River Corridor
attractions.description: The Heart River Corridor is a significant recreational and environmental area in Mandan, North Dakota, where the Heart River flows into the Missouri River. This corridor integrates critical flood protection infrastructure with a network of multi-use trails, offering scenic views of river-bottom woodlands and opportunities for nature-based activities like hiking and biking. It serves as a vital link in the region's trail system, connecting local neighborhoods to major historical and cultural sites. (City of Mandan Strategic Plan, Lower Heart River Water Resource District)

attractions.name: Mandan Rodeo Days
attractions.description: Mandan Rodeo Days is one of the world's oldest continuously running rodeos, celebrating its 147th anniversary in 2026 with a legacy dating back to 1879. This premier professional event attracts over 500 top cowboys and cowgirls to compete in PRCA and WPRA sanctioned performances, including bull riding, bronc riding, and barrel racing. The multi-day celebration features theme nights, specialty acts, and a spectacular fireworks display, making it the cornerstone of the region's Independence Day festivities.

highlights: ['PRCA and WPRA sanctioned professional rodeo featuring world-class athletes.', 'Themed nightly performances: Family Night, Patriot Night, and Fireworks Night.', 'A massive fireworks display following the final performance on July 4th.', 'Free "Slack" performance on July 1st with all-day contestant competition.', "Family-friendly entertainment including Mutton Bustin' and the Junior Job Site construction play area.", 'On-site amenities like Vendor Row, a diverse food truck area, and beer gardens.']
attractions.tips: Tickets should be purchased in advance as seating at the Dale Pahlke Arena is all-reserved and limited. Visitors should arrive early to enjoy the food trucks and vendors, and note that outside food, beverages, and firearms are prohibited. Tobacco use is also restricted within 20 feet of arena facilities.

attractions.name: Missouri River State Natural Area

It offers nearly five miles of single-track trails for hiking and mountain biking, providing a rare forest experience in the region. Visitors can enjoy the solitude of the riparian habitat, which is home to diverse wildlife including bald eagles, whitetail deer, and wild turkeys. (North Dakota Parks & Recreation, Wanderlog)

attractions.name: On-A-Slant Indian Village (Fort Abraham Lincoln State Park)
attractions.description: On-A-Slant Indian Village is a 400-year-old Mandan settlement that once consisted of approximately 85 earthlodges and a population of 1,500 people. It served as a major trade hub for over two centuries before being decimated by a smallpox epidemic in 1781. Today, it is part of Fort Abraham Lincoln State Park and features six reconstructed earthlodges that offer visitors insight into the sedentary lifestyle and culture of the Mandan people (North Dakota Parks and Recreation, National Park Service).
